card.indications: Adjunct to diet and exercise for improving glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes. It is used alone or with other glucose-lowering therapy.
card.mechanism: Reversibly inhibits intestinal alpha-glucosidase enzymes, delaying carbohydrate digestion and absorption and reducing the post-meal rise in blood glucose. It does not stimulate insulin release.
card.dosing: Commonly start with 25 mg by mouth three times daily with the first bite of each main meal. Titrate gradually according to postprandial glucose, A1C, and gastrointestinal tolerance. Maximum dose depends on body weight and product labeling.
card.routeAdministration: Take with the first bite of each main meal. Skip the dose when a meal is skipped. Titrate slowly to reduce gastrointestinal effects.
card.contraindications: Diabetic ketoacidosis; cirrhosis; inflammatory bowel disease, colonic ulceration, partial intestinal obstruction or predisposition to obstruction; chronic intestinal disease associated with marked digestion or absorption problems; or conditions worsened by increased intestinal gas.
card.warnings: May cause elevated liver enzymes, especially at higher doses. Hypoglycemia does not occur when used alone but may occur with insulin or sulfonylureas; treat with oral glucose rather than sucrose because carbohydrate breakdown is delayed.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Flatulence, abdominal discomfort, diarrhea, bloating, and increased bowel sounds. Effects are most common early in therapy and often improve with continued use and gradual titration.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hepatotoxicity, marked transaminase elevation, intestinal obstruction, pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis, and severe hypoglycemia when combined with other agents.
card.interactions: Insulin and sulfonylureas increase hypoglycemia risk. Digestive enzyme products and intestinal adsorbents may reduce effectiveness. Some drugs that raise glucose can reduce control. Review all diabetes medicines when doses change.
card.monitoring: Check fasting and postprandial glucose, A1C, gastrointestinal tolerance, and liver enzymes during the first year and periodically thereafter according to product guidance. Monitor for hypoglycemia when combined with insulin or a secretagogue.
card.patientTeaching: Take with the first bite of food. Follow the prescribed meal plan and exercise program. For low blood sugar, use glucose tablets or gel, not table sugar or candy made mainly with sucrose. Report severe abdominal pain, persistent diarrhea, jaundice, or dark urine.
card.nutrition: Works best with a consistent carbohydrate-controlled diet. Large amounts of sucrose may worsen gastrointestinal effects. Carry a rapid source of pure glucose when also using medicines that can cause hypoglycemia.

card.indications: Treatment and suppression of herpes simplex infections, treatment of varicella-zoster infections, and selected prophylactic uses in immunocompromised patients. Topical products are used for limited mucocutaneous herpes indications.
card.mechanism: Converted by viral thymidine kinase to active metabolites that inhibit viral DNA polymerase and terminate viral DNA chain elongation, limiting viral replication in infected cells.
card.dosing: Regimens vary widely by infection, route, immune status, age, and renal function. Oral therapy is commonly given several times daily; IV therapy is weight based. Dose reduction is required with renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Begin as early as possible in the course of infection. Maintain hydration. Infuse IV doses over at least 1 hour and never give as an IV bolus. Shake oral suspension well. Apply topical products only to the prescribed area and avoid the eyes unless the product is specifically ophthalmic.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to acyclovir or valacyclovir. Use caution and adjust dosing in renal impairment, dehydration, older adults, and patients receiving other nephrotoxic drugs.
card.warnings: IV or high-dose therapy can cause acute kidney injury from crystal precipitation, neurotoxicity, and thrombotic microangiopathy in susceptible patients. Rapid IV administration increases renal risk.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, headache, malaise, vomiting, local infusion-site irritation, and burning or stinging with topical application.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute kidney injury, crystalluria, encephalopathy, seizures, hallucinations, severe skin reactions, anaphylaxis, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura/hemolytic uremic syndrome in immunocompromised patients.
card.interactions: Other nephrotoxic drugs increase kidney risk. Probenecid and cimetidine may increase exposure. Mycophenolate and other renally eliminated drugs may have altered concentrations. Review renal function and concurrent medications.
card.monitoring: Assess renal function, hydration status, urine output, neurologic status, infection response, lesion progression, and IV site. Monitor CBC and renal tests more closely with prolonged, high-dose, or IV treatment.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed and start early. Therapy controls outbreaks but does not eradicate latent virus or prevent all transmission. Maintain hydration, avoid sexual contact when lesions are present, and report reduced urine output, confusion, tremors, severe rash, or persistent symptoms.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Encourage adequate fluid intake unless restricted. Food may reduce stomach upset without meaningfully reducing effectiveness.

card.indications: Short-term management of anxiety disorders, relief of anxiety symptoms, and treatment of panic disorder with or without agoraphobia.
card.mechanism: Enhances gamma-aminobutyric acid activity at GABA-A receptors, producing central nervous system inhibition, anxiolysis, sedation, and anticonvulsant effects.
card.dosing: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest appropriate duration. Immediate-release dosing is usually divided; extended-release dosing is generally once daily. Older adults and patients with hepatic impairment require lower initial doses. Taper gradually after regular use.
card.routeAdministration: Give exactly as prescribed. Do not crush, chew, or split extended-release tablets. Measure concentrate accurately. Avoid abrupt discontinuation after repeated use.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines and concurrent use with strong CYP3A inhibitors such as ketoconazole or itraconazole. Use extreme caution with severe respiratory disease, substance-use history, depression, and older adults.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings address profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death with opioids; abuse, misuse, and addiction; and physical dependence with potentially life-threatening withdrawal. Abrupt withdrawal can cause seizures.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, impaired coordination, fatigue, memory difficulty, slowed thinking, lightheadedness, constipation, and dry mouth.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory depression, severe oversedation, falls, paradoxical agitation, suicidal thoughts or behavior, dependence, misuse, withdrawal seizures, delirium, and coma.
card.interactions: Opioids, alcohol, antihistamines, sleep medicines, muscle relaxants, antipsychotics, and other CNS depressants increase sedation and respiratory risk. Strong CYP3A inhibitors increase alprazolam levels; inducers may reduce effectiveness.
card.monitoring: Assess anxiety or panic symptoms, level of consciousness, respiratory rate, oxygenation, gait, falls, mood, suicidality, misuse risk, and adherence. Monitor closely during dose changes and tapering.
card.patientTeaching: Do not drink alcohol, use opioids, or take sedating medicines unless specifically approved. Do not drive until effects are known. Take only as prescribed, store securely, never share, and do not stop suddenly. Seek help for extreme sleepiness, slowed breathing, confusion, or suicidal thoughts.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid grapefruit products if advised because they may increase drug exposure. Avoid alcohol completely during therapy.

dosageForms: Cathflo 2 mg vial for catheter clearance

card.indications: Thrombolysis for eligible acute ischemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, and massive pulmonary embolism. A separate low-dose product is used to restore function to occluded central venous access devices.
card.mechanism: Binds fibrin within a thrombus and converts plasminogen to plasmin, which degrades fibrin and dissolves the clot.
card.dosing: Dosing is indication specific and often weight based. Acute ischemic stroke commonly uses 0.9 mg/kg, maximum 90 mg, with 10% as an initial bolus and the remainder over 60 minutes. MI, PE, and catheter-clearance regimens differ and must follow the exact protocol.
card.routeAdministration: Confirm indication, weight, time of symptom onset, imaging, blood pressure, laboratory eligibility, and contraindications before administration. Use the correct product and reconstitution method. Avoid unnecessary punctures, injections, and invasive procedures during and after treatment.
card.contraindications: Active internal bleeding and conditions with high risk of catastrophic bleeding. Stroke-specific exclusions include intracranial hemorrhage, recent intracranial surgery or serious head trauma, uncontrolled severe hypertension, and other protocol-defined risks.
card.warnings: Major or fatal bleeding, especially intracranial hemorrhage, can occur. Orolingual angioedema, cholesterol embolization, and reperfusion arrhythmias may occur. Anticoagulants and antiplatelet drugs increase bleeding risk.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Bleeding or oozing from puncture sites, bruising, nausea, mild hypotension, and reperfusion arrhythmias in myocardial infarction.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Intracranial hemorrhage, gastrointestinal or retroperitoneal bleeding, fatal hemorrhage, severe angioedema, anaphylaxis, cardiac rupture after MI, and systemic embolization.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, NSAIDs, and other drugs affecting hemostasis increase bleeding risk. ACE inhibitors may increase the risk of orolingual angioedema.
card.monitoring: Continuous neurologic, cardiac, respiratory, and hemodynamic monitoring is required. Assess for sudden headache, vomiting, acute hypertension, neurologic decline, bleeding, angioedema, and reperfusion. Follow protocol-directed CBC, coagulation studies, imaging, and post-thrombolysis restrictions.
card.patientTeaching: Explain the urgent purpose and bleeding risk when time permits. After treatment, report sudden headache, weakness, speech change, chest pain, abdominal or back pain, swelling of the tongue or lips, or any bleeding immediately.
card.nutrition: Keep NPO during acute stroke evaluation until swallowing is assessed. Avoid oral intake or invasive tubes when bleeding risk and neurologic status make them unsafe. Resume nutrition according to the acute-care protocol.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible bacterial infections involving the ear, nose, throat, respiratory tract, genitourinary tract, skin, and other sites; used in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori.
card.mechanism: Binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis, causing cell lysis in susceptible organisms.
card.dosing: Dose and duration depend on infection, severity, age, weight, renal function, and local guidance. Adult regimens commonly range from 250 to 875 mg per dose at scheduled intervals. Renal impairment may require interval or dose adjustment.
card.routeAdministration: Give at evenly spaced times and complete the prescribed course. May be taken with or without food. Shake suspension well and measure accurately; refrigerate when product instructions recommend it.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to amoxicillin, penicillins, or another beta-lactam antibiotic. Use caution with prior severe cephalosporin allergy, renal impairment, and infectious mononucleosis because rash is common.
card.warnings: Serious anaphylaxis and severe skin reactions can occur. Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea may appear during therapy or weeks later. Prolonged use can cause superinfection.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, rash, headache, and oral or vaginal candidiasis.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome or toxic epidermal necrolysis, DRESS, severe C. difficile colitis, interstitial nephritis, hemolytic anemia, seizures with high levels, and hepatic injury.
card.interactions: Warfarin effect and INR may increase. Allopurinol increases rash risk. Probenecid increases amoxicillin concentrations. Live oral typhoid vaccine effectiveness may decrease.
card.monitoring: Assess infection response, allergy history, bowel pattern, rash, renal function when indicated, and signs of superinfection. Monitor INR more closely in patients taking warfarin.
card.patientTeaching: Take every dose and finish the course even when feeling better. Report hives, breathing difficulty, blistering rash, severe or bloody diarrhea, or signs of yeast infection. Do not use leftover antibiotics or share medication.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food to reduce stomach upset. Maintain hydration. Yogurt or probiotics may be discussed with the healthcare team but do not prevent all antibiotic-associated diarrhea.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension, angina pectoris, and selected patients after acute myocardial infarction. It is also used off label for certain tachyarrhythmias and other cardiovascular conditions.
card.mechanism: Selectively blocks beta1-adrenergic receptors at usual doses, lowering heart rate, contractility, renin release, and myocardial oxygen demand.
card.dosing: Hypertension often starts at 25 to 50 mg by mouth once daily and may increase to 100 mg daily. Dosing after MI and for other conditions follows specific protocols. Reduce dose or extend interval in renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Check apical pulse and blood pressure before giving. Give consistently at the same time each day. Do not discontinue abruptly, especially in patients with coronary disease.
card.contraindications: Sinus bradycardia, heart block greater than first degree without a pacemaker, cardiogenic shock, and overt or decompensated heart failure. Use caution with asthma, diabetes, peripheral vascular disease, and renal impairment.
card.warnings: Abrupt withdrawal can worsen angina, trigger myocardial infarction, or cause arrhythmias. Bradycardia, hypotension, heart block, and worsening heart failure may occur. Beta blockade can mask tachycardia from hypoglycemia and hyperthyroidism.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Fatigue, dizziness, bradycardia, cold extremities, depression, nausea, and exercise intolerance.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe bradycardia, heart block, hypotension, cardiogenic shock, worsening heart failure, bronchospasm, and severe hypoglycemia without typical warning symptoms.
card.interactions: Other rate-slowing drugs such as verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, amiodarone, and clonidine increase bradycardia or conduction risk. NSAIDs may reduce antihypertensive effect. Insulin and sulfonylureas may have masked hypoglycemia symptoms.
card.monitoring: Monitor heart rate, blood pressure, ECG when indicated, signs of heart failure, renal function, glucose in patients with diabetes, and frequency of angina or arrhythmia symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Take consistently and do not stop suddenly. Learn how to check pulse and blood pressure when instructed. Rise slowly. Report fainting, very slow pulse, shortness of breath, swelling, rapid weight gain, or worsening chest pain.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Follow heart-healthy and sodium-restricted guidance when prescribed. Avoid excessive alcohol because it may worsen dizziness and hypotension.

card.indications: Reduction of LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, treatment of dyslipidemia, and reduction of cardiovascular events in appropriate adults and pediatric patients with selected lipid disorders.
card.mechanism: Inhibits hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, decreasing cholesterol synthesis and increasing LDL-receptor activity, which lowers circulating LDL and other atherogenic lipoproteins.
card.dosing: Adults commonly receive 10 to 80 mg by mouth once daily. Intensity is selected according to cardiovascular risk, baseline LDL, response, age, and tolerance. Pediatric dosing uses lower approved ranges.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily at any time, with or without food. Use a consistent schedule. The suspension requires product-specific preparation and measurement instructions.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless a specialist determines otherwise under current product guidance. Use caution with active liver disease and significant unexplained transaminase elevations.
card.warnings: Myopathy and rhabdomyolysis can occur, especially with interacting drugs, higher doses, older age, renal impairment, or hypothyroidism. Hepatic injury and increased glucose or A1C may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Muscle aches, joint pain, diarrhea, nausea, headache, nasopharyngitis, and mild elevation of liver enzymes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury, immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy, severe hepatic injury, and serious hypersensitivity reactions.
card.interactions: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors, cyclosporine, certain antivirals, clarithromycin, azole antifungals, fibrates, colchicine, and large amounts of grapefruit may increase myopathy risk. Review all medicines before changes.
card.monitoring: Obtain baseline lipid profile and liver tests. Recheck lipids after initiation or dose changes and periodically thereafter. Assess muscle symptoms, weakness, dark urine, diabetes risk, and CK or liver tests when clinically indicated.
card.patientTeaching: Continue diet, exercise, and risk-factor reduction. Report unexplained muscle pain or weakness, fever, dark urine, severe fatigue, or jaundice. Tell providers about all new medications and pregnancy plans.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Follow a heart-healthy eating pattern low in saturated and trans fats. Avoid excessive grapefruit intake and limit alcohol because of interaction and liver concerns.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible respiratory, skin, sexually transmitted, and other bacterial infections; selected prophylactic or mycobacterial uses; ophthalmic treatment for susceptible eye infections.
card.mechanism: Binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis. It is usually bacteriostatic but may be bactericidal at higher concentrations against susceptible organisms.
card.dosing: Regimens vary by infection and formulation. Common adult oral regimens include 500 mg on day 1 followed by 250 mg daily on days 2 through 5, or 500 mg daily for 3 days. IV and pediatric dosing are indication and weight based.
card.routeAdministration: Give immediate-release tablets and suspension with or without food; food may improve tolerance. Extended-release suspension has different food instructions. Reconstitute and infuse IV doses according to product guidance; do not give as IV push. Use ophthalmic drops with clean technique.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to azithromycin, erythromycin, or another macrolide/ketolide; history of cholestatic jaundice or hepatic dysfunction associated with prior azithromycin use.
card.warnings: QT prolongation and torsades de pointes can occur, especially with existing QT risk, low potassium or magnesium, bradycardia, or interacting drugs. Severe hepatic injury, C. difficile diarrhea, allergic reactions, and worsening myasthenia gravis may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, vomiting, headache, and local infusion or eye irritation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Torsades de pointes, sudden cardiac death, liver failure, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, severe C. difficile colitis, hearing changes, and exacerbation of myasthenia gravis.
card.interactions: Other QT-prolonging drugs increase arrhythmia risk. Aluminum- or magnesium-containing antacids can reduce peak levels of immediate-release oral doses if taken together. Warfarin effect may increase. Digoxin and other substrates may require monitoring.
card.monitoring: Assess infection response, bowel pattern, liver symptoms, ECG and electrolytes in high-risk patients, hearing changes with prolonged therapy, and IV or ophthalmic site tolerance.
card.patientTeaching: Complete the full course. Report fainting, palpitations, severe diarrhea, jaundice, dark urine, severe rash, breathing difficulty, or hearing changes. Do not use for viral infections or share antibiotics.
card.nutrition: Most immediate-release oral forms may be taken with food to reduce nausea. Follow the specific instructions for extended-release suspension. Maintain hydration during diarrhea or fever.

card.indications: Reduction of spasticity associated with multiple sclerosis, spinal cord disease, or other selected neurologic conditions. Intrathecal therapy is reserved for severe spasticity unresponsive to oral treatment or causing intolerable adverse effects.
card.mechanism: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release and decreasing muscle spasm, clonus, and pain associated with spasticity.
card.dosing: Oral treatment commonly starts at 5 mg three times daily and increases gradually every few days to the lowest effective dose. Intrathecal dosing requires screening and pump-specific specialist management. Reduce dosing in renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses on a consistent schedule, with food if needed. Do not stop abruptly. Intrathecal baclofen must be delivered only through the appropriate pump or catheter system by trained clinicians.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. Intrathecal administration is contraindicated in the presence of infection at the injection site. Use caution with renal impairment, seizure disorders, psychiatric illness, and patients who rely on some spasticity for posture or function.
card.warnings: Abrupt withdrawal, especially from intrathecal therapy, can cause high fever, rebound spasticity, altered mental status, rhabdomyolysis, organ failure, and death. Overdose can cause profound CNS and respiratory depression.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, weakness, fatigue, nausea, constipation, headache, and hypotonia.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory depression, coma, seizures, severe hypotonia, aspiration, psychiatric changes, urinary retention, and life-threatening withdrawal syndrome.
card.interactions: Alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, sleep medicines, and other CNS depressants increase sedation and respiratory risk. Antihypertensives may increase hypotension.
card.monitoring: Assess spasticity, pain, mobility, muscle strength, sedation, respiratory status, falls, renal function, bowel and bladder function, and signs of overdose or withdrawal. Intrathecal pump patients require refill and device monitoring.
card.patientTeaching: Do not stop suddenly or miss pump refill appointments. Avoid alcohol and driving until effects are known. Report unusual weakness, breathing difficulty, extreme sleepiness, confusion, fever, severe rebound spasms, or pump alarms immediately.
card.nutrition: Take with food if nausea occurs. Maintain hydration and fiber because constipation may worsen. Avoid alcohol.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension, heart failure, left ventricular dysfunction after myocardial infarction, and diabetic nephropathy in selected patients.
card.mechanism: Inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme, lowering angiotensin II and aldosterone, reducing vasoconstriction and sodium retention, and increasing bradykinin.
card.dosing: Dosing varies by indication. Hypertension often starts at 25 mg two or three times daily, with lower doses in patients receiving diuretics, older adults, or renal impairment. Heart failure and post-MI regimens begin lower and titrate carefully.
card.routeAdministration: Give on an empty stomach 1 hour before meals because food reduces absorption. Check blood pressure, renal function, and potassium. Do not use during pregnancy.
card.contraindications: Prior ACE-inhibitor-related angioedema, hereditary or idiopathic angioedema, pregnancy, concurrent aliskiren in patients with diabetes, and use with sacubitril/valsartan within 36 hours.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for fetal toxicity. Angioedema, severe hypotension, hyperkalemia, renal failure, neutropenia, and rare hepatic failure may occur. Risk increases with renal artery stenosis, dehydration, renal disease, or potassium-raising drugs.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry cough, dizziness, hypotension, fatigue, rash, altered taste, nausea, and mild increases in potassium or creatinine.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Angioedema with airway obstruction, acute kidney injury, severe hyperkalemia, neutropenia or agranulocytosis, fetal injury or death, and severe hypotension.
card.interactions: Potassium supplements, salt substitutes, potassium-sparing diuretics, ARBs, aliskiren, NSAIDs, lithium, and neprilysin inhibitors can cause serious interactions. Diuretics may increase first-dose hypotension.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, renal function, potassium, volume status, heart-failure symptoms, and urine protein when used for nephropathy. Check CBC in patients at risk for neutropenia.
card.patientTeaching: Take 1 hour before meals and do not stop without guidance. Avoid potassium salt substitutes unless approved. Report swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or throat; difficulty breathing; fainting; fever; sore throat; reduced urine; or pregnancy immediately.
card.nutrition: Take on an empty stomach. Follow sodium restriction when prescribed. Avoid excessive potassium intake or potassium-containing salt substitutes unless the prescriber specifically approves them.
